topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: In modern democracy, a government is _________
options: a contrast between the rulers and the ruled
a preserve of the political party in power
alternation of power between the majority and minority tribes
explanation: In democratic systems, there is a distinction between those who govern (the rulers) and the citizens who are governed (the ruled). This contrast emphasises the relationship and accountability mechanisms between the government and the people, ensuring that those in power are elected and held accountable by the electorate.

subject: Government
q: The ability to make and enforce laws and administer justice constitute the major functions of a _________
explanation: The government is responsible for creating legal frameworks, enforcing laws and ensuring justice within a society. These functions are central to its role in maintaining order and governance.

subject: Government
q: Executive power in a cabinet system of government is vested in the ________
explanation: In a cabinet system of government, executive power is vested in the council of ministers (also known as the cabinet). The cabinet, led by the Prime Minister or Head of Government, is responsible for the executive functions of the government, including implementing laws and running government departments. The legislature, on the other hand, is responsible for making laws.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: A feature of a confederal state is that _______
options: it is a single sovereign state
any unit can secede at will
its powers are distributed equally between the centre and units
explanation: In a confederal system, the central authority is relatively weak compared to the member states or units, which retain significant sovereignty and autonomy. This often includes the right of the units to secede if they choose to do so.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: One of the limitations to the principles of rule of law is _________
explanation: Immunity, such as legal immunity granted to certain individuals (e.g., heads of state, diplomats) or entities, can limit the application of the rule of law by exempting certain people from legal accountability and protections that apply to others.

subject: Government
q: Countries adopt the unitary system of government because it __________
options: concentrates power only at the centre
allows the  component units to secede at will
explanation: In a unitary system, power is centralised at the national level, which can help foster a sense of unity and national identity by ensuring consistent policies and laws across the entire country. This centralisation can help integrate diverse regions and populations into a cohesive national framework.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is an advantage of monocephalous executive ___________
explanation: A monocephalous executive, where power is concentrated in a single individual (such as a president or a monarch), allows for more streamlined and faster decision-making compared to systems where power is distributed among multiple individuals or bodies.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: A major difference between power and authority is that, authority is ________
explanation: Authority is often granted or recognised based on legal, institutional or social norms and is accepted by the people it governs. It is legitimate and accepted by those subject to it, whereas power can be exercised through various means, including coercion or force, and does not necessarily require popular acceptance.

subject: Government
q: Political participation can take the form of __________
options: being a member of a pressure group
explanation: Canvassing for votes involves actively campaigning and encouraging people to vote for a particular candidate or party, and it is a direct way to participate in the political process.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a disadvantage of proportional representation?
options: makes for easy decision making in parliament
is difficult and cumbersome to operate
explanation: Proportional representation can be complex to administer due to the need for accurate vote counting and the allocation of seats in proportion to the number of votes received. This complexity can lead to administrative challenges and potential delays in determining election outcomes.

subject: Government
q: The sum total of authority in a state unrestrained by law is _______
explanation: An oligarchy is a form of government where power is concentrated in the hands of a small group of individuals or families, and their authority may be unrestrained by broader legal or democratic principles.

subject: Government
q: The flexibility and rigidity of the constitution refer to the __________
explanation: A flexible constitution can be amended relatively easily, often through ordinary legislative processes. In contrast, a rigid constitution requires more complex and stringent procedures for amendments, making it more difficult to change.

subject: Government
q: The observance of Rule of Law in a state requires _________
explanation: An independent judiciary ensures that laws are applied impartially and that legal rights are protected without undue influence from other branches of government or external pressures. This is essential for maintaining the Rule of Law, which requires that everyone, including the government, is subject to and accountable under the law.

subject: Government
q: The principle of separating the judiciary from the other organs of government and making it free from executive and legislative influence is termed __________

subject: Government
q: The right to respect the personality of the individual is a _________
explanation: Natural rights are fundamental rights that are considered inherent and universal, applying to all individuals by virtue of their humanity. They include the right to personal dignity and respect, which is central to the concept of natural rights.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following constitutional provisions gives a high degree of autonomy to component parts of a unitary state?
explanation: Devolution involves the delegation of powers and responsibilities from the central government to regional or local authorities, allowing them some level of self-governance while remaining part of a unitary state. This is distinct from a federal system, where sovereignty is constitutionally divided between central and regional governments.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: The following are redresses available at the courts for aggrieved people except _________
explanation: Prorogation refers to the discontinuation of a session of a legislative body, which is not a legal remedy or redress available through the courts. The other options—injunction, mandamus, and certiorari—are legal remedies used to address grievances related to legal and administrative matters.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following usually leads to the curtailment of the rights of citizens?
explanation: During periods of violent unrest or conflict, governments may impose restrictions on civil liberties, such as freedom of speech, assembly and movement, often in the name of maintaining public order and security.

subject: Government
q: The national secretariat of a political party is managed by the ________
explanation: The national chairman and general secretary are key officials responsible for the administration, organisation and management of the party's national secretariat.

subject: Government
q: The type of election which allows party members to elect candidates for elective offices is _________
explanation: Primary elections are held within political parties to select their candidates for general elections. These elections determine which candidate will represent the party in the main electoral contest.

subject: Government
q: An opposition party which sees itself as ''government -in-waiting'' is called ______
explanation: A shadow government is typically formed by the main opposition party and consists of members who would take over ministerial roles if the party were to win the next election.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: The document which sets out the programme of a political party and guides the electorate to vote for it is the ________
explanation: The document which sets out the program of a political party and guides the electorate to vote for it is the manifesto. A manifesto outlines a party's policies, objectives and plans, helping voters understand what the party stands for and what it aims to achieve if elected.

subject: Government
q: An advantage of a two-party system is that it __________
options: protects the electorate from votr buying
explanation: A two-party system often makes the electoral process more straightforward, with clear choices for voters and simplified voting outcomes. It can reduce the complexity associated with multiple parties and coalitions and lead to more decisive and manageable electoral results.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a non-partisan organisation with the aim of influencing government policies?
explanation: A pressure group is defined as an organized group, which influences government decisions socially or economically, without necessarily entering into election activities for the control of the government.

subject: Government
q: The collective views held by majority of citizens of a country about a particular policy is _________
explanation: Public opinion reflects the general attitudes and beliefs of the population on various issues, including policies and government actions.

subject: Government
q: An institution under the executive arm of government responsible for formulating and implementing government policies is the ________
explanation: The civil service is composed of professional staff employed by the government to manage the day-to-day operations of government departments and to implement policies formulated by the executive branch.

subject: Government
q: The following are means of controlling public corporation except _________
explanation: Public corporations are typically controlled through mechanisms such as legislative control, judicial control and ministerial control. The chief of staff is not a standard mechanism for controlling public corporations.

subject: Government
q: The institution established by the constitution to be responsible for recruiting personnel into the civil service is the ___________
explanation: The Public Service Commission, or Civil Service Commission is typically responsible for recruiting, hiring, and managing personnel within the civil service based on established guidelines and procedures. This commission ensures that recruitment processes are fair, transparent, and based on meritocracy.

subject: Government
q: If a winner emerges in an election as a result of his/her votes being more than the combined votes of the opposing contestants, what types of electoral system is being practised?
explanation: If a winner emerges in an election as a result of having more votes than the combined votes of the opposing contestants, the type of electoral system being practised is Absolute majority. In an absolute majority system, a candidate must receive more than 50% of the total votes to win. This ensures that the winner has a clear majority of support.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a function of an electoral commission?
options: disciplining personnel in the civil service
explanation: Electoral commissions are responsible for organising and overseeing elections, which includes tasks such as preparing and maintaining electoral registers, ensuring the integrity of the voting process and managing the logistics of the election. Adjudicating election petitions, disciplining personnel in the civil service and promoting public servants are not typical functions of an electoral commission.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is not an electoral malpractice?
explanation:
Electoral malpractices are illegal or unethical practices that undermine the fairness of elections. Examples include:

- Impersonation: Voting in someone else's name.
- Threatening: Coercing or intimidating voters or candidates.
- Gerrymandering: Manipulating electoral district boundaries to favour a particular party or group.

Campaigning for a candidate, on the other hand, is a legitimate part of the electoral process and is not classified as malpractice.

subject: Government
q: Local government sources of revenue can best be improved through?
explanation: Cottage industries can provide a significant boost to local economies by creating jobs, generating income, and increasing local revenue through taxes and business rates. Supporting and developing small-scale, local industries helps to diversify and strengthen the financial base of local governments.

subject: Government
q: Public opinion is important in that, it ________
options: helps political parties to assess their policies
creates delays in the execution of policies
can be manipulated by political opponents
explanation: By gauging public opinion, political parties and policymakers can understand the views and preferences of the electorate, which helps them to shape and adjust their policies to better align with the needs and concerns of the public. This feedback is crucial for effective governance and political strategy.

subject: Government
q: One of the features of pressure groups is that all members ________
explanation: Pressure groups are formed by individuals or organisations with common goals or interests, and they work together to influence public policy or decision-making in ways that benefit their shared objectives.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following modes of operation of pressure group undermines the economic, social and political stability of the state ________
explanation: Strikes, particularly when widespread or prolonged, can disrupt economic activities, create social tensions and lead to political instability, as they often involve protests against government policies or business practices.

subject: Government
q: The police perform all the following functions except ________
explanation: While the police are involved in enforcing the law, prosecuting lawbreakers, protecting life and property, and maintaining law and order, they are not responsible for public execution. Public executions, if legal and authorized, are typically carried out by designated executioners or personnel specifically tasked with that responsibility, not by the police. Therefore, public execution of lawbreakers would not be part of police functions.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a reason for the failure of the assimilation policy in French West Africa?
options: The change in name of their culture
The refusal of the African to be culturally colonized
The paternal policy of the French
Bribery of the opinion leaders by the French
explanation: The assimilation policy aimed to integrate African colonies into French culture and society. However, many Africans resisted this cultural assimilation, preferring to maintain their own cultural identities and traditions. This resistance was a significant factor in the policy's failure.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The capital of the French Federation of West Africa states was located in?
explanation: The capital of the French Federation of West Africa states was located in Dakar, Senegal. Dakar served as the administrative centre for the federation, which was a collective grouping of French colonies in West Africa.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: Under the crown colony system of administration, major policies were implemented in the colonies by the ________
options: Secretary of states for the colonies
explanation: In the crown colony system, the Colonial Governor had significant authority and was responsible for overseeing the administration and implementation of policies in the colony. This system allowed the British government to exert direct control over the colony's governance.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: One way of improving productivity in the public service is through?
explanation: One way to improve public service productivity is through higher salaries and allowances. Providing competitive salaries and allowances can boost morale, attract skilled personnel and incentivise employees to perform better. This leads to increased productivity in the public service.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following made the colonial Government a " Virtual Dictator"?
explanation: Reserved veto powers allowed the colonial government to override or reject decisions made by local legislative or executive bodies, giving it significant control and authority over the governance of the colony. This effectively allowed the colonial administration to act with considerable autonomy and minimal local input.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration III - Putting it all together...

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is a feature of the French policy of Association?
options: Elevation of the position of the chiefs
explanation: The policy of Association aimed to integrate the colonies more closely with France by granting certain privileges such as French citizenship to some colonial subjects. This contrasted with the policy of Assimilation, which sought to integrate colonies into French culture and political systems fully.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The African Union is most often unable to resolve conflicts because of _________
explanation: Language barriers and race contribute significantly to misunderstandings and difficulties in communication, coordination and cooperation among member states, thereby hindering effective conflict resolution. Cultural differences do play a role, but they are generally considered within the broader context of language and race.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The formation of the African Union is aimed at achieving a wider goal of ________
explanation: The African Union seeks to promote a sense of collective identity and solidarity among African nations and to foster unity and cooperation across the continent.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: A major advantage of a country's foreign policy is that, it __________
options: encourages infringement on the territories of other states
stimulates political activities in the state
promotes understanding between the leaders and the led
explanation: The primary goal of a country's foreign policy is to safeguard and promote its national interests, including security, economic prosperity and international influence. A responsible foreign policy does not encourage infringement but rather aims for peaceful coexistence.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The West African Students Union (WASU) was formed by _______
explanation: Chief Ladipo Solanke was a Nigerian lawyer and nationalist who founded the West African Students' Union in 1925 in London. WASU was created to advocate for the rights of West African students studying in the United Kingdom and to promote the broader interests of West Africans under colonial rule.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: A military coup d'etat is followed by _________
options: breaking of diplomatic relations with foreign countries
explanation: After a military coup, the constitution is typically suspended as the military junta takes control, bypassing constitutional norms and procedures.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Which of the following countries is a unitary state?
explanation:
The country that is a unitary state is Egypt. Egypt is a unitary state, where the central government holds the majority of the power, and any administrative divisions (such as governorates) have only the powers that the central government chooses to delegate.

Nigeria, Ghana and Canada are federal states, meaning they have multiple levels of government (federal, state, and local) with shared powers and responsibilities.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following does not influence a country's foreign policy?
explanation:
The factor that does not influence a country's foreign policy is the age of the leader. Economic considerations, such as attracting foreign investments, international law, conventions and treaties and cultural factors, including religion and race, can play a significant role in foreign policy decisions.

While the experience, personality, and vision of a leader can influence foreign policy, the leader's age alone is not a direct factor in shaping a country's foreign policy. It is the leader's policies, decisions and diplomatic strategies that matter, not their age.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The National Congress of British West Africa (NCBWA) was founded in _______
explanation: The National Congress of British West Africa was founded in 1920 by Joseph Ephraim Casely Hayford of Gold Coast (Ghana) and Dr Akinwande Savage of Nigeria. It was founded as a political organization representing the interests of the educated elite in the British West African colonies. Although the organisation included representatives from other British West African colonies such as Sierra Leone, Nigeria and the Gambia, the founding meeting and much of its initial leadership were based in the Gold Coast.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q:
(a) What is Political socialization?

(b) identity four features of a nation
explanation:
(a) Political socialization is defined as the process of acquiring political beliefs, values, attitudes and citizenship training that shapes a citizen's political behaviour.

Political socialization can be defined as the way in which people acquire values and opinions that shape their political stance and ideology.

(b) (i)Permanence: A nation remains permanent. However, the Government can change many times.

     (ii)A defined territory: A nation has a defined territory with a limit to its size. It must have clearly boundaries, separating it from others.

     (iii) Government: Although the government is not a permanent feature of a nation, it remains a significant feature because it is machinery set up by the nation to steer the affairs of the people in the nation.

     (iv) Sovereign: The sovereign of a nation is not negotiable and must be free from external interference and control. The nation has the supreme power to make decisions and enforces the same on the people.

     (v) Population: This is an important attribute of a nation. There must be a given number of people making up a nation.

     (vi) Recognition: A nation must be recognized both internally and externally.

subject: Government
q: Highlight five features of federalism?
explanation:
(i) Constitutional division of powers: Powers are constitutional shared between the central authority and other subordinate units, e.g state and local government 

(ii) Derivation of powers from the constitution: The central, state and local governments derive their powers from the constitution

(iii) Supremacy of the constitution: The constitution of a Federal state is powerful and supreme.

(iv) Written and Rigid: The constitution of most federal systems are written and rigid

 (v) Separation of powers: The constitution separate the functions and powers among three organs of government, namely: executive, legislature, and judiciary

(vi) Bicameral legislature: Most of the federal constitutions have bicameral legislature. For example, in Nigeria and the USA, there are the senate and the House of Representatives.

(vii) The supreme court: There is always a supreme court that interprets the constitution and decides on any conflict between the Centre and the constituents units

(viii) Equal representation: Every interest group is recognized and represented in federalism.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q:
(a) What is a Public Corporation?

(b) Identify four ways in which Public corporations are controlled.
explanation:
(a) A public corporation is an entity that is created by the government to carry out certain public missions and services.

A pubic corporation is defined as the government establishment or enterprise set up by an Act of parliament to provide essential services like electricity, postal services, pipe-borne water etc

(b) (I) Legislative control: Legislative control or Acts of parliament: Public corporations are set up by Acts of parliament. Their power is stipulated in the Acts. Abolition: Any public corporation that is deficient and unproductive could be abolished by the legislature. Auditing: Any accounts of the public corporations are always audited by a body set up by the legislature. Summoning of officials: The legislature can ask any official to appear before it if there are allegations of wrongdoing.

(ii) Ministerial control: Dissolution of the board of directors: A minister whose ministry controls a public corporation can appoint and dissolve a board of directors of such a corporation. Appointment and dismissal of senior officers: The minister also has the power to appoint or even dismiss some senior categories of staff in any corporation under his control. Approval of loans and expenditures: The minister approves any loan to be taken and some major expenditures. Auditing: A minister can appoint auditors to audit the accounts of public corporations under his or her control.

(iii) Judicial control: Judicial control of court order: The courts can declare any act made by some corporations illegal and unconstitutional. This is done when they are breaching the acts upon which they are set up. Denial of services: The judiciary can also stop any public corporation from denying its services to any customer e.g. PHCN

(iv) Financial control: Finance: Most public enterprises depends, to a large extent, on the government for allocations to meet their running costs. The government may withhold such allocations. Budget: This is usually debated upon. The senior official or the minister responsible for the enterprise can be questioned before the legislative committee. Annual Report: Annual reports of public corporations are presented to the parliament for scrutiny.

subject: Government
q: Outline five ways in which Public opinion is being measured?
explanation:
(i) Channels of communication: Public opinion can be determined by exploring all the channels of communication, e.g. opinions expressed in Newspapers, radio, television etc.

(ii) Opinions expressed by leaders of thought: It can be measured through opinions expressed by local leaders of thought or leaders in the representative assemblies.

(iii) Letters to government: It can be measured through the letters written by citizens to the government agencies expressing their opinions on particular issues of national importance.

(iv) Response to government policies: It can be measured through mass protests and demonstrations for or against government programmes.

(v) Referendum or Plebiscite: Government may conduct a referendum or plebiscite to determine what the people want on any particular issue.

(vi) Telephone calls: Phone-in programmes on radio and television stations, especially in Nigeria, are a great way of measuring public opinion

subject: Government
q: Explain five means of safeguarding rights of citizens?
explanation:
(i) Obedience of Law: The law of the land should be obeyed

(ii) Educating the citizens: Citizens should be educated on their rights.

(iii) Punishing the rights violators: The violators of the rights of citizens should be punished severely by the court of law

(iv) Separation of powers: There should be adherence to the theory of separation of powers among the organs of government.

(v) Sufficient remuneration of judges: There should be sufficient salaries and remunerations of judges, and these should be from the consolidated funds.

(vi) Immunity of judiciary: There should be immunity of the judiciary so that they can safeguard the rights of citizens.

(vii) Promotion of judges: Judges should be promoted when due, and the promotion should not be from the executive arm of government.

(viii) Independence of the judiciary: Judges must be totally free from every control and interference of the other arms of government.

subject: Government
q: Identify five challenges faced by the United Nations Organizations (UNO)
explanation:
(i) Shortage of funds: the involvement of the United Nations Organization in large-scale operations results in a shortage of fund

(ii) Sovereignty of nations: The organization cannot effectively enforce its decisions because member states are not willing to surrender their sovereignty to its authority.

(iii) The veto power: Each of the five permanent members of the Security Council has veto power. The refusal of any of them on any issue under consideration.

(iv) Differences in ideology: There are differences in ideology, culture, politics and methods used in pursuing economic goals. This may negate the evolution of lasting peace in the world.

(v) Difficulty in achieving the goals of the organization: The achievement of the goals of collective security can be jeopardized by negative action by any of the Big- Powers - USA, Russia, Britain, France and China- determining the direction of events in the world.

(vi) No permanent Military force: The absence of a permanent military force is affecting its peace-keeping operations worldwide. The member countries provide troops only when called upon.

(vii) Problem of arms race: The UN has failed to find a permanent solution to the arms race, Many nations spend so much on the manufacture of weapons of war, e.g USA, Russia, France, Britain, Even some countries like Iraq and Iran equally do the same thing.

(viii) The presence of some regional/sub-regional groupings: some of these groupings like AU, ECOWAS, EEC, and the presence of some military pacts or alliances, e.g. NATO, WARSAW, and ECOMOG, have weakened commitment to the ideals and objectives of the UN

(ix) International Monetary Fund (IMF): It is an agency of the UN. Its neo-colonialist policies and conditionalities have crippled the economies of most states globally, especially the developing ones.

(x) Constant border clashes/disputes: These are common among member nations, e.g India and Parkistan, France and Madagascar, etc.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: Highlights five differences between the crown colony and the protectorates?
explanation:
(i) Crown Colony is a form of direct rule of British annexed territories in which the British established political institutions such as the executive council, legislative council and governor which were used in ruling the people who were regarded as British subjects.

Protectorate is a region, political unit or state that is controlled and protected by another or government by which it is ruled

(ii) Crown colony is a part of a larger country governed by that same country.

A protectorate is its own state merely protected by a larger country.

(iii) For a colony, all foreign relations are created and dealt with by the mother country.

A state which is a protectorate has the aid of a larger nation when conducting foreign relations

(iv)The governors are virtual colonial dictators as they have veto powers.

The people in a more equal relationship are governed through their traditional rulers and chiefs.

(v) Unlike a protectorate, a colony is not in an equal relationship with its mother country.

A protectorate is in a more equal relationship with its mother country than a colony

(vi) There is the existence of the monarch (the king or queen) who is the head of all the British colonies.

There is the existence of traditional rulers and chiefs ruling the people subject to the control by the governor on behalf of the monarch.

(vii) Colonies can be seen as a threat to other nations, as it is the spreading of one country to other countries.

Protectorates can be seen as an alliance. The more allies, the stronger the territory and country.

(viii) A colony is governed internally and externally by a foreign power.

A protectorate governs itself internally, while the foreign power controls its external relations.

(ix) It is far more expensive to govern.

Unlike the crown colony, it is not that expensive to govern.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q:
(a) List three political parties and their leader who contested during the 1979 presidential elections in Nigeria

(b) Highlight the features of political parties in Nigeria since independence.
explanation:
(a) (i) The National Party of Nigeria (NPN) - Shehu Shagari

     (ii) Unity Party of Nigeria - Chief Obafemi Awolowo

     (iii) Nigeria People's Party (NPP) - Dr Nnamdi Azikwe

     (iv) People's Redemption Party (PRP) - Mallam Aminu Kanu

     (v) Great Nigeria People's  Party (GNPP) Alhaji Ibrahim Waziri

(b) (I) Unity: Political parties unite the people on major issues affecting the state

(ii) Serving as a link: They always serve as a link between the people and the government

(iii) Accountability: They enable the government to become accountable to the electorate through constructive criticism of the government in power.

(iv) Machinery for recruitment: They provide machinery for recruiting political leaders.

(v) Clarification of issues: Political parties try to clarify issues and provide relevant information on candidates and current events, thereby encouraging the electorate to be more interested in governmental activities.

(vi) Change of government: Change of government is made possible with ease as a result of political parties contesting elections.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Highlight five weaknesses of the 1999 constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria.
explanation:
(i) Costly to run: The constitution operates a presidential system of government with comprehensive duplication of political functions, thereby making it too expensive and costly to run.

(ii) Abuse of impeachment clause: The impeachment clause as stipulated in the constitution could be abused by the lawmakers to victimise a political opponent.

(iii) Delay in implementation of programmes: Separation of powers could result in a delay in the implementation or carrying out of government programme. For example, the National Assembly can delay  the approval of budget, ministerial and other appointments to be made by the president.

(iv) Refusal of the executive to honour the judgement of the supreme court: the executive can refuse to honour the judgement of the supreme court because of the sovereign immunity it has.

(v) Activities in government: Government activities could be brought to a standstill because of problems between the executive and legislative arms of government, especially when the ruling party does not have a majority seat in parliament.

(vi) Over- concentration of powers: Some critics have argued that the Executive president as both Head of State and Government has excessive powers concentrated in him, and this creates room for abuse of powers.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q:
(a) List three features of the Igbo Political system.

(b)Identify four democratic tenets of the Igbo Traditional Political system.
explanation:
(a) (i) Religious and Judicial control: The village priest/ diviner wielded a great deal of religious and judicial powers.

(ii) Leadership: Succession to leadership and to prominence was based on personal achievements

(iii) Bonds and Unity: Deities are of political significance in providing bonds and unity.

(iv) Market Square: It served as the village chambers.

(v) Socio-political group: The women (Umuada) composed of the first daughter of each family, constituted a socio-political group.

(vi) Banishment: Banishment from the village and escort of such people into the evil forest was the maximum judicial punishment.

(vii) New yam festival: This was religiously marked, honoured and celebrated annually.

(viii) Decentralized administration: Different authorities, groups and institutions took part in the administration.

(b) (I) The executive: the affairs of the village were discussed and executed from time to time by family heads (council of elders) who formed the executive arm

(ii) The legislature: The people in this society made laws themselves, and even the age-grades could enact a law that would be accepted by the elders.

(iii) The judiciary: The family heads championed the settlement of disputes between families, Serious cases were referred to the council of elders presided over by the Okpara and penalties were meted out to those who committed serious offenses like murder, stealing, etc.

(iv) The Ozo titleholders: Some wealthy and influential men took this title which was very expensive. This title made the holder recognized in the society and could then preside over meetings on issues affecting the community with elders.

(v) Consensus: Critical issues before the authorities at the village assembly were reached by consensus.

(vi) Council of elders: There was the existence of a council of elders (Ama-ala) presided over by the eldest son in the village.

(vii) Value of individual personality: In a democracy, the belief in the value of individual personality is restored. It implies the need to respect the other man, to listen to his arguments and take into account his point of view.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: An undesirable attribute of capitalism is mostly seen in
options: efficient production of goods and services
explanation: Exploitation of labour refers to the tendency in capitalist systems for workers to receive less value than the worth of what they produce. This often leads to economic inequality and poor working conditions.

subject: Government
q: A constitution that requires an absolute majority of members of parliament to amend is a
explanation: A rigid constitution requires a more complex process to amend, often involving a significant majority in the legislature, and sometimes a referendum or approval from multiple institutions.

subject: Government
q: What is the term given to the action of discontinuing a session of a parliament or other legislative assembly without dissolving
explanation:
Prorogation is the formal ending of a parliamentary session by the head of state or other authority, without dissolving the parliament. It marks the end of a session but not the end of the legislative body's existence.

Adjournment refers to the suspension of a sitting of a legislative body to a later date or time. Recess refers to a break or temporary halt in the proceedings of a legislative body, not the discontinuation of a session.

subject: Government
q: In a monocephalous executive system of government, all executive powers are vested in the
explanation: In a monocephalous executive system, such as in a presidential system, all executive powers are concentrated in a single individual, the President.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: The action programme of a political party intended to be carried out when political power is won is known as
explanation: A manifesto is a public declaration by a political party outlining its proposed policies, plans and goals that it aims to implement if it wins the election.

subject: Government
q: What does the mass media rely on to gauge public opinion?
explanation: Polls and surveys are structured methods used by the mass media to collect data on public opinion. This allows for a quantifiable assessment of how the public feels about various issues. Influential citizens, propaganda, interviews, rumours and gossip are not objective or reliable means of gauging the opinion of the public.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following options is the role of a newspaper in a state?
options: Ensures fair distribution of relief items
explanation: Newspapers play a critical role in disseminating information to the public, including news, analysis and opinion, thereby informing citizens about various aspects of governance and society.

subject: Government
q: The Civil Services helps the government to
options: stay in power for a long time.
explanation:
Civil Services, which include various government agencies and departments staffed by civil servants, are responsible for formulating, developing, and implementing government policies. Civil servants are often experts in their respective fields, and they provide the necessary knowledge, expertise, and administrative support to policymakers.

The role of Civil Services in policy development and implementation contributes to the overall governance and functioning of the state. Their expertise helps governments address complex issues, manage resources, and respond to the needs of the society they serve. While the elected officials set the direction and priorities, civil servants play a vital role in translating these into actionable policies and programs.

subject: Government
q: A citizen is barred from active politics when employed as a
explanation: Civil servants are usually barred from engaging in active politics to ensure that they remain impartial and non-partisan in their roles

subject: Government
q: Citizens' political participation is best practised under a
explanation: Political parties are the primary organisations through which citizens engage in the political process. They offer a platform for political participation, including voting, running for office and influencing policy and governance.

subject: Government
q: The most important function of government is to ensure the
options: second arm of government is respected
explanation: The primary and most crucial function of government is to ensure the safety and security of its citizens, maintaining law and order and protecting them from internal and external threats.

subject: Government
q: The right to an adequate standard of living falls under
explanation: Economic rights include the right to an adequate standard of living, which encompasses access to essential resources like food, shelter and healthcare.

subject: Government
q: People's political values are continuously shaped by the following agents except
explanation: An ombudsman is an official appointed to investigate complaints against government agencies or officials. While important for addressing grievances and ensuring accountability, an ombudsman does not typically shape people's political values.

subject: Government
q: In which political system were there two levels of government with considerable devolution?
explanation: Federalism is a system of government in which power is divided between a central (national) government and regional (state or provincial) governments. This division of powers allows for considerable devolution of authority to the regional governments, enabling them to have a degree of autonomy in certain matters. In a federal system, each level of government has its own set of responsibilities and powers.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: A system of government in which people elect their lawmakers and make them accountable for their actions and inactions in government is
options: checks and balances system of government
explanation: In a representative government, citizens elect officials to make decisions and govern on their behalf. These elected representatives are accountable to the electorate, who can vote them out of office if they fail to perform effectively.

subject: Government
q: Which territories in British West Africa did the colonial government exercise limited jurisdiction?
explanation: In protectorates, the British colonial government exercised limited jurisdiction, allowing local rulers to retain some authority while British officials managed foreign policy and major administrative functions. This system provided a degree of autonomy to local governments under British oversight.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: One of the following French West African colonies was the first to gain independence
explanation: Guinea was the first French West African colony to gain independence, achieving this on October 2, 1958. It chose to leave the French Union in a referendum, making it the earliest of the French colonies to become independent. Benin, Burkina Faso and Togo gained independence in 1960.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The struggle by the chiefs and people of West Africa against political, social and economic dominance by European powers was referred to as
explanation: Nationalism is the term used to describe the movement for political independence and self-determination, where people of West Africa sought to resist and overthrow colonial rule and assert their own national identity and sovereignty.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The European imperialist penetration into West Africa was primarily motivated by the following considerations except
explanation: Compassion was not a primary motivator for European imperialism in West Africa. The motives for imperialism were more centred around economic gain, social and political influence, and military strategy.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: Which policy made the colonised French West African partners with France in her colonial project?
explanation: While the assimilation Policy (19th century – early 20th century) aimed to turn colonized Africans into French citizens if they adopted French culture, the association Policy (introduced in the mid-20th century) acknowledged that the people of French West Africa had distinct cultures and should not be forced to become "French." Instead, it promoted political participation and granted more autonomy to local leaders. This shift was crucial in redefining Africans as partners rather than subjects in the French colonial empire.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The British colonial governor in West Africa wielded sweeping powers by virtues of
explanation: The veto power allowed the British colonial governor to override or reject legislation passed by the local legislative bodies or councils, giving them substantial control over colonial policies and governance.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The action plan by African countries under the New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) aimed at tackling the following issues except
explanation: NEPAD does not specifically address religious issues. Its primary focus is on economic, political and social development rather than religious matters.

explanation: In a one-party system, the ruling party controls all political power and there is usually no room for opposition or dissent. This often leads to authoritarian or dictatorial rule where power is concentrated in the hands of a single party or leader.

explanation: Frequent military coups undermine democratic institutions, disrupt the rule of law and erode the legitimacy of elected governments. This leads to a loss of democratic progress and stability and makes it difficult to build or sustain democratic governance.
