q: The biggest obstacle which also slowed down nationalist activities in French West African Colonies was the
options: constitutional reforms and entrenchment of human rights
explanation: The biggest obstacle which also slowed down nationalist activities in French West African Colonies was the assimilation policy. This policy aimed to make Africans in French colonies 'French' in culture and citizenship, which undermined local cultures and identities and hindered the development of nationalist movements.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: A state that has two levels of government is commonly referred to as
explanation: A state that has two levels of government is commonly referred to as a federal state. In a federal state, power is divided between a central government and individual states, provinces, or other territorial units. Each level of government has its own set of responsibilities and powers.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: One of the major criticisms levelled against the United Nations (UN) is its inability to
options: enforce affirmative action and equal wealth
adopt a universal language for its operation
explanation: One of the major criticisms levelled against the United Nations (UN) is its inability to ensure fair permanent representation. The UN Security Council, for example, has five permanent members with veto power, which many countries argue is not representative of the international community.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: One of the function of the electoral management body is to
options: conduct training programmes for presidential candidates
look for external sources besides the government to fund its operations
engage in business to increase its financial capacity
explanation: One of the functions of the electoral management body is to interpret the electoral laws fairly. This is to ensure that elections are conducted in a free, fair, and transparent manner. The electoral management body does not engage in business or look for external sources of funding.

subject: Government
q: The civil service is different from the public service in terms of
explanation: The civil service is different from the public service in terms of conditions of service. The civil service is a part of the public service, but it is distinguished by its different conditions of service, which include things like pay scales, working hours, and benefits.

subject: Government
q: The judiciary occupies an important place in every state because it is
options: mandated to supervise the functions of the executive
protector of the rights of the people
a body of upright and just people in society
explanation: The judiciary is important in every state because it serves as the protector of the rights of the people. It interprets the law and ensures that everyone, including the government, adheres to it. The other options do not accurately describe the primary role of the judiciary.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: An executive president is one who wields the powers of a
explanation: An executive president is one who wields the powers of both the head of state and government. This means that the president has the highest executive authority and is responsible for the implementation and enforcement of all laws and the operation of all government services.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: There are two types of committee system in the House of parliament as part of their operations and one of such is
explanation: There are two types of committee systems in the House of Parliament as part of their operations. One of such is the Committee of the Whole House. This is a committee that includes all members of the House and meets in the House chamber.

subject: Government
q: A democratic country is one in which the people have the right to
options: punish government by not honouring tax obligation
explanation: A democratic country is one in which the people have the right to participate in decision making. This is a fundamental principle of democracy, which is government of the people, by the people, and for the people. The other options like disregarding laws they abhor, violently overthrowing a government, and punishing government by not honouring tax obligation are not democratic rights and can lead to anarchy and instability.

subject: Government
q: The best way to keep the military at bay from interfering in politics is by ensuring
options: effective control of their day to day operations
explanation: The best way to keep the military at bay from interfering in politics is by ensuring good governance. Good governance, which includes things like transparency, accountability, and respect for the rule of law, can help to prevent the conditions that might lead to a military coup.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The African Union (AU) is saddled with one of these challenges and that is
options: tensions and political discord among member states
inter-ethnic extinction and poor social cohesion
explanation: The African Union (AU) is an organization that promotes unity and cooperation among African countries. One of the challenges it faces is tensions and political discord among member states. This can hinder cooperation and the achievement of the AU's objectives. The other options do not accurately describe the challenges faced by the AU.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The reluctance of military regimes to hand over power to civilian administration is because they
options: negotiate better loan terms for the country than the civilian government
have the backing of traditional rulers, their subjects and the clergy
perceive civilians and politicians as evil doers and backward
explanation: Military regimes often feel a sense of entitlement to power due to their control over the armed forces and the state's security apparatus. This sense of entitlement can make them reluctant to hand over power to civilian administrations. The other options do not accurately capture the primary reason for this reluctance.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The exercise of power can arise from any of the following sources except
explanation: The exercise of power can arise from legitimate power, charismatic power, and expert power. However, ideological power is not a source of power. It is rather a tool or mechanism through which power can be exercised.

subject: Government
q: A social, political and economic arrangement in which landed property and other natural resources are collectively owned is referred to as
explanation: Communalism refers to a social, political and economic arrangement in which landed property and other natural resources are collectively owned. It is a system that emphasizes the common good rather than individual profit.

subject: Government
q: Public opinion is formed through the following channels except
explanation: Public opinion is formed through various channels such as mass media, elections, and political parties. However, the cabinet, which is a body of high-ranking state officials, typically consisting of the top leaders of the executive branch, is not a channel through which public opinion is formed. Rather, it is a body that makes decisions based on public opinion and other factors.

subject: Government
q: The function of the Diplomatic Missions include the following except
options: project the culture of the people
pay salaries of workers of the mission
explanation: The function of the Diplomatic Missions include gathering intelligence information, projecting the culture of the people, and wooing investors. However, paying salaries of workers of the mission is an administrative function, not a diplomatic one.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: One of the factors that influences decentralization of power in a state is
explanation: Decentralization of power in a state is influenced by the need to widen political participation. This is because decentralization allows for the distribution of authority away from a central authority to regional or local authorities, thereby allowing more people to participate in governance.

subject: Government
q: One of the high points of the activities of the National Congress of British West Africa (NCBWA) led to the
options: creation of the African High Command
explanation: One of the high points of the activities of the National Congress of British West Africa (NCBWA) led to the introduction of the elective principles. The NCBWA was a political organization that advocated for the rights of Africans in British West Africa, and one of its major achievements was the introduction of the elective principle, which allowed Africans to vote for their own representatives.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The pattern of rule by the French in her West African colonies was highly
explanation: The French colonial rule in West Africa was highly centralized. This means that the power and decision-making were concentrated in a central authority, often in the hands of the colonial governor. The other options do not accurately describe the pattern of French colonial rule.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The Commonwealth of Nations is committed to one of the following principles
options: supervise political activities of member states
explanation: The Commonwealth is an intergovernmental organization of 54 member states that are mostly former territories of the British Empire. It is committed to promoting human rights and democracy among its member states. The other options do not accurately describe the principles to which the Commonwealth is committed.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: The cornerstone of a functioning democracy is the
explanation: The cornerstone of a functioning democracy is free and fair elections. This is because in a democracy, the power to govern comes from the people who are governed. They express their will through voting in elections. If the elections are not free and fair, then the government does not truly represent the people, and the system is not a true democracy.

subject: Government
q: The issues that are at the core of a country's foreign policy are referred to as
explanation: The issues that are at the core of a country's foreign policy are referred to as national interest. National interest is a country's goals and ambitions whether economic, military, or cultural. It is a key concept in international relations where it refers to a country's goals and ambitions.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The underlying principle of the concept of seperation of powers is mainly to
explanation: The principle of separation of powers is primarily to prevent arbitrariness. This principle is a model for the governance of a state, where the state's government is divided into branches, each with separate and independent powers and areas of responsibility so that the powers of one branch are not in conflict with the powers associated with the other branches.

subject: Government
q: Secret voting assists the voter in making a decision without
explanation: Secret voting is a method of voting that ensures the voter's privacy in the election process. The key idea is to ensure the voter's choice is confidential which helps to prevent attempts of influence, coercion, or retaliation

subject: Government
q: Elections plays a major role in the democratic process because, it
explanation: Elections play a major role in the democratic process because they confer legitimacy. By participating in elections, citizens give their consent to be governed by the elected officials, which is a key aspect of democratic legitimacy. The other options do not accurately describe the major role of elections in the democratic process.

subject: Government
q: Which one among the following is a significant factor that influences the foreign policy of a country?
explanation: The foreign policy of a country is significantly influenced by its political system. The political system of a country determines its governance structure, ideologies, and international relations. It shapes the country's approach to international diplomacy, alliances, and conflicts. The other options like educational system, gender of the political leader, and recruitment policy do not have a direct and significant influence on a country's foreign policy.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: One of the negative effects of colonial rule on West African states was the
options: building of roads and rail network
explanation: One of the negative effects of colonial rule on West African states was the dependency syndrome. This refers to the condition where the colonized states become heavily dependent on the colonial powers for economic, political, and social needs. The other options are generally considered as positive effects of colonial rule.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration III - Putting it all together...

subject: Government
q: One of the low points in the cabinet system of government is that, the
options: divided loyalty of the cabinet ministers is strong
centre of power in the system is stable
Prime Minister does not regard the Head of State
tenure of government is always not stable
explanation: In a cabinet system of government, one of the low points is that the tenure of government is not always stable. This is because the government is dependent on the confidence of the parliament, and a vote of no confidence can lead to the dissolution of the government.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Identify five functions of government as an Agent of the State
explanation:
The government, as an agent of the state, performs several functions. These include:
- Law-making: The government formulates and implements laws to guide the conduct of citizens.
- Maintenance of Law and Order: The government ensures peace and security through law enforcement agencies.
- Provision of Public Goods and Services: The government provides essential services such as education, healthcare, and infrastructure.
- Protection of Rights and Liberties: The government protects the fundamental rights and freedoms of its citizens.
- Economic Management: The government manages the economy through fiscal and monetary policies to ensure economic stability and growth.

subject: Government
q: Explain five factors that hamper the effective operation of the justice delivery system
explanation:
Several factors can hamper the effective operation of the justice delivery system. These include:
- Corruption: This undermines the integrity of the justice system.
- Inadequate Funding: Lack of sufficient funds can lead to delays and inefficiencies.
- Poor Infrastructure: Inadequate facilities can hinder the administration of justice.
- Inadequate Manpower: A shortage of trained personnel can affect the delivery of justice.
- Political Interference: This can compromise the independence of the judiciary.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Highlight Five factors that have contributed to the weakened government institutions in many West African States
explanation:
Several factors have contributed to the weakening of government institutions in many West African States. These include:
- Corruption: This undermines the effectiveness and credibility of government institutions. - Political Instability: Frequent changes in government can disrupt the functioning of institutions.
- Poor Governance: Lack of transparency and accountability can weaken institutions.
- Inadequate Funding: Lack of resources can hinder the performance of institutions.
- Weak Rule of Law: This can undermine the authority and effectiveness of institutions.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Institutions of Government in Post-Independence Nigeria

subject: Government
q: In what five ways does political socialization assist in the democratic process of a state?
explanation:
Political socialization assists in the democratic process of a state in several ways. These include:
- Formation of Political Opinions: Through political socialization, individuals develop their political beliefs and opinions.
- Civic Participation: It encourages citizens to participate in the political process.
- Development of Political Culture: It helps in the development of a democratic political culture.
- Promotion of Political Stability: It promotes political stability by inculcating in citizens the values of tolerance and compromise.
- Facilitation of Political Change: It facilitates peaceful political change by preparing citizens to accept new political ideas and leaders.

subject: Government
q: Outline five reasons for which it is important for a state to have efficient local government system
explanation:
It is important for a state to have an efficient local government system for several reasons. These include:
- Grassroots Development: Local governments are closer to the people and can therefore address their specific needs.
- Promotion of Democracy: Local governments provide a training ground for democracy as they involve people in decision-making.
- Efficient Service Delivery: Local governments can deliver services more efficiently due to their proximity to the people.
- Conflict Resolution: Local governments can help resolve local disputes and maintain peace.
- Economic Development: Local governments can stimulate economic development by implementing local economic policies.

subject: Government
q: Explain five reasons for which the study of nationalism and nationalist activities is important to the democratic development of a state
explanation: The study of nationalism and nationalist activities is important to the democratic development of a state for several reasons. Firstly, it fosters a sense of unity and common identity among the citizens of a state. This unity is crucial for the smooth functioning of a democratic system as it promotes cooperation and mutual understanding among the diverse groups within the state. Secondly, nationalism encourages active participation in the democratic process. When citizens feel a strong sense of belonging to their nation, they are more likely to take part in elections, voice their opinions, and engage in other democratic activities. Thirdly, nationalism can serve as a bulwark against external threats and influences. A strong sense of national identity can help to protect the democratic institutions of a state from foreign interference. Fourthly, nationalism can promote social cohesion and stability, which are essential for the effective functioning of a democratic system. Lastly, the study of nationalism and nationalist activities can provide valuable insights into the historical, cultural, and social factors that shape the democratic development of a state.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Identify four qualities of a good political leader
explanation: A good political leader should possess several qualities. Firstly, they should have integrity. This means they should be honest, transparent, and accountable in their actions. Secondly, they should have good communication skills. They should be able to articulate their ideas clearly and persuasively, and they should also be good listeners. Thirdly, they should have a vision. They should have a clear idea of what they want to achieve and how to go about it. Fourthly, they should have the ability to inspire and motivate others. They should be able to rally their followers and inspire them to work towards a common goal.

topics.title: Social Dynamics and Classical Political Thought

subject: Government
q: What is political leadership?
explanation: Political leadership refers to the role of individuals or groups in influencing, guiding, and directing the political activities and affairs of a state or a political entity. It involves making decisions that affect the lives of the citizens, formulating policies, implementing laws, and maintaining order and stability in the society. Political leaders can be elected officials, such as presidents and prime ministers, or they can be influential figures in political parties, social movements, or other political organizations. The effectiveness of a political leader is often measured by their ability to mobilize support, achieve their political objectives, and contribute to the overall well-being of the society.

topics.title: Social Dynamics and Classical Political Thought

subject: Government
q: In what five ways is diplomacy important to the development of Nigeria?
explanation: Diplomacy plays a crucial role in the development of Nigeria in several ways. Firstly, it helps to maintain peace and security. Through diplomatic negotiations and agreements, Nigeria can resolve conflicts with other countries and prevent the outbreak of war. Secondly, diplomacy enables Nigeria to establish and maintain economic ties with other countries. This can lead to increased trade, investment, and economic growth. Thirdly, diplomacy allows Nigeria to gain international support for its development goals. By engaging in diplomatic activities, Nigeria can persuade other countries and international organizations to provide financial aid, technical assistance, and other forms of support. Fourthly, diplomacy can enhance Nigeria's international reputation and influence. By acting as a responsible and constructive member of the international community, Nigeria can gain respect and influence on the global stage. Lastly, diplomacy can help to protect the rights and interests of Nigerians abroad. Through diplomatic channels, the Nigerian government can assist its citizens who are living, working, or traveling in other countries.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Outline five positive political impacts colonial administrations had on West African States leading to statehood
explanation: Colonial administrations had several positive political impacts on West African States leading to statehood. Firstly, they introduced modern systems of governance, including the principles of rule of law and administrative efficiency. Secondly, they established infrastructure such as roads, railways, and ports, which facilitated trade and communication. Thirdly, they introduced formal education, which produced a class of educated Africans who later became leaders in the struggle for independence. Fourthly, they brought about economic development, albeit uneven, through the introduction of cash crops and mining. Lastly, they inadvertently fostered a sense of nationalism among Africans. The experience of colonial rule, with all its injustices and inequalities, made Africans more aware of their common identity and their desire for freedom and self-determination.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration III - Putting it all together...

subject: Government
q: Identify five mistakes committed in democratic governance that give cause for the military to intervene in politics
explanation:
Democratic governance is a form of government where the supreme power is vested in the people and exercised by them directly or indirectly through a system of representation usually involving periodically held free elections. However, there are instances where democratic governance fails due to certain mistakes, which may give cause for the military to intervene in politics. Here are five such mistakes:

- Corruption: This is one of the most common mistakes in democratic governance. When the elected officials engage in corrupt practices, it undermines the trust of the people in the government and can lead to military intervention to restore order and integrity.

- Abuse of Power: When those in power use their position to serve their personal interests rather than the interests of the people, it can lead to dissatisfaction among the populace and create a vacuum that the military may feel compelled to fill.

- Failure to uphold the rule of law: Democratic governance is based on the rule of law. When this is not upheld, it can lead to chaos and disorder, providing a reason for the military to step in.

- Political Instability: Frequent changes in government, political infighting, or lack of clear leadership can lead to instability, which can be detrimental to the nation's progress. In such cases, the military may intervene to establish stability.

- Infringement on Human Rights: When a democratic government fails to protect the rights of its citizens or engages in activities that infringe on these rights, it can lead to public outcry and potential military intervention.

In conclusion, while democratic governance is the preferred form of government, certain mistakes can undermine its effectiveness and give cause for the military to intervene. However, it's important to note that military intervention in politics is generally viewed as a setback to democratic governance and is not a desirable outcome.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The ultimate source of power and authority which sets the state apart from other forms of political organisations is referred to as
explanation: Sovereignty is the supreme power and authority of a state to govern itself without external interference. It is what distinguishes a state from other political organisations, as it grants the state ultimate control over its internal and external affairs.

subject: Government
q: The institution through which leaders exercise power to make and enforce laws is the
explanation: The government is the institution through which leaders exercise their power to make and enforce laws. It is responsible for creating policies, maintaining order, and ensuring that laws are followed within a state. The judiciary, political parties and security agencies are integral parts of the political system, but the government is the overarching institution that exercises the authority to legislate and enforce laws.

subject: Government
q: The fundamental principles by which a state is ordered and governed are usually outlined in a
explanation: A constitution is a set of fundamental principles or established precedents that govern the state. It outlines the structure of government, the distribution of powers, the rights of citizens and the laws that guide the functioning of the state. It serves as the supreme legal document that shapes how a state is ordered and governed.

subject: Government
q: Which one among the following factors greatly restrains the government in a democratic society?
options: Consent of the civil society organisation
explanation: In a democratic society, the government is greatly restrained by the consent of the governed. This principle means that the legitimacy of the government and its actions are based on the approval or consent of the people. If the government fails to meet the expectations of the people, they have the power to challenge it, through elections or other democratic means, thus limiting its authority and actions.

subject: Government
q: The following factors are challenges faced by the judiciary under the doctrine of Separation of Powers except
explanation:
The doctrine of Separation of Powers divides government responsibilities into three branches — Executive, Legislature, and Judiciary — to prevent abuse of power and ensure checks and balances. When discussing challenges faced by the judiciary under this doctrine, the focus is on issues that affect judicial independence and balance of power.

Judges are often appointed by the executive (e.g. President or Governor), which can compromise judicial independence if the process lacks transparency. If the judiciary does not control its own finances but depends on the executive for funding, it undermines its independence. When the executive or legislature has excessive influence over how judges are removed, it threatens judicial independence.

But, while poor legal infrastructure is a real challenge for the judiciary, it is not directly related to the doctrine of separation of powers. It is more of a logistical or administrative issue.

subject: Government
q: Accountability in constitutional democracy is best enforced through a system of
explanation: In a constitutional democracy, accountability is best enforced through a system of checks and balances. This system ensures that no single branch of government (executive, legislature, or judiciary) becomes too powerful. Each branch has the ability to limit the actions of others, preventing abuses of power and promoting accountability. While civil society organizations and the selection of the right leaders are important, checks and balances are the primary mechanism for enforcing accountability within the structure of government.

subject: Government
q: The application of the common principles that are important to improve decision-making processes and ultimately strengthen democracy and improve social and economic development is best described as
explanation: Good governance refers to the application of principles such as transparency, accountability, participation, rule of law and effectiveness in the decision-making processes. These principles are crucial for strengthening democracy, improving social and economic development and ensuring that governments operate in a way that serves the best interests of the people.

subject: Government
q: One of the reasons for having two legislative chambers in the governance structure of a country is to ensure the
options: executive is kept under check and control
powers of the lower chamber is suppressed
strict control of the affairs of the state
explanation: Having two legislative chambers, commonly known as a bicameral legislature, allows for a thorough scrutiny of legislation. This structure ensures that proposed laws are carefully reviewed and debated by both chambers, providing a system of checks and balances. The process allows for more comprehensive consideration of issues, preventing hasty decisions and ensuring that legislation is in the best interest of the country.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Which one of the following factors has contributed largely to the weakened and poor performance of state institutions in most West African States?
explanation: Corruption is one of the major factors contributing to the weak and poor performance of state institutions in most West African states. It undermines governance, leads to the misallocation of resources, weakens public trust and reduces efficiency in service delivery. Corruption manifests in various ways, including bribery, embezzlement, nepotism and mismanagement of public funds.

subject: Government
q: One of the paramount roles to play as a citizen in sustaining the democratic system of governance is to
explanation: One of the most fundamental responsibilities of a citizen in a democracy is participating in elections. By registering and voting, citizens actively shape the government, hold leaders accountable and influence policies that affect their lives. Voting ensures that leaders are chosen through a legitimate, democratic process rather than through undemocratic means.

subject: Government
q: The responsibility of citizens towards nation-building is to
options: encourage civil disobedience in the face of hardship
participate and contribute to decision making
boycott and withdraw services to the state
explanation: Citizens play a crucial role in nation-building by actively engaging in governance and contributing to decision-making processes. This includes voting, engaging in policy discussions, advocating for good governance and holding leaders accountable. A participatory democracy ensures that policies reflect the needs and interests of the people.

subject: Government
q: By competing in general elections, political parties ultimately offer citizens
options: opportunity to be extremely to one party
explanation: Political parties compete in general elections to provide citizens with different policy options, leadership choices and governance approaches. By doing so, they give voters the power to decide which party or candidates best represent their interests. This competition is essential for democracy, as it ensures that governance is based on the will of the people rather than a single dominant group.

subject: Government
q: Public opinion is an essential element in a democracy because it can act as a
options: check on leadership and government activities
news outlet for the media and government only
propaganda tool for the government at all material times
explanation: Public opinion is a fundamental aspect of democracy because it helps hold leaders accountable and ensures that government actions reflect the will of the people. Through protests, petitions, elections and the media, citizens express their views, influencing policy decisions and governance. Governments that ignore public opinion risk losing legitimacy and public trust.

subject: Government
q: The media serve a useful purpose in a democracy by
options: connecting the government and the people
ensuring government plays to their dictates
assisting in the formation of political parties
explanation: In a democracy, the media plays a crucial role by acting as a bridge between the government and the people. They provide information about government policies, decisions and activities while also amplifying the voices of citizens. This helps create transparency, accountability and informed public participation in governance.

subject: Government
q: One of the pivotal roles played by pressure groups in a functioning democracy is the
options: implementation of public policy by them
trumpeting of good deeds of a government always
blackmailing of the government to act in their favour
explanation: Pressure groups play a crucial role in a democracy by advocating for specific interests and influencing government policies. They achieve this by lobbying, organizing protests, engaging in public campaigns and using the media to shape public opinion. Their goal is to ensure that government decisions reflect the needs and concerns of different segments of society.

subject: Government
q: Promoting electoral integrity is the crucial function of which institution of state?
explanation: The electoral management body is the institution responsible for organising, conducting and overseeing elections to ensure they are free, fair, and credible. It establishes electoral guidelines, monitors compliance, and ensures transparency in the electoral process. In Nigeria, for example, the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) performs this role.

subject: Government
q: These rights which are explicitly protected by the Constitution are referred to as
explanation: Entrenched clauses refer to provisions in the Constitution that are explicitly protected and cannot be easily altered or abolished without following a special procedure. It usually requires a supermajority vote or a referendum for amendment. These clauses protect fundamental rights and liberties.

subject: Government
q: Constitutionally, the local government administration is controlled by the state through
explanation: In most countries, including Nigeria, local government administrations are constitutionally controlled by the state through legislation. This means that state governments have the authority to pass laws that define and regulate the operations, functions and powers of local government areas within their jurisdiction.

subject: Government
q: Which state institution is referred to as the bureaucratic wing responsible for the running of government offices, agencies and departments?
explanation: The Civil Service is the bureaucratic wing responsible for the administration and management of government offices, agencies and departments. It comprises non-political, permanent government employees who are tasked with implementing government policies and ensuring the smooth functioning of public services.

subject: Government
q: The local government administration in many West African countries is faced with a myriad of problems one of which is
explanation: One of the key challenges faced by local government administrations in many West African countries is employment retention. This refers to the difficulty of maintaining a stable and motivated workforce within local government institutions, due to factors such as low wages, lack of career development opportunities and political interference.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: One of the following options  does not pass as a means of ensuring the independence of the judiciary which is
explanation: For the judiciary to remain independent, its members must be free from undue influence or control, especially from the executive or legislative branches. Salary determined by the executive compromises this independence, as it could lead to political pressure or manipulation. In contrast, immunity from prosecution, promotion on merit, and security of tenure are all important safeguards to ensure that judges can carry out their duties impartially and without fear of reprisal.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: As part of its constitutional mandate, the Public Service Commission operates to
options: appoint the head of the commission
carry out the order of the presidency only
recruit high ranking personnel for the civil service
review salaries and allowances of staff of the executive only
explanation: The Public Service Commission is responsible for overseeing the recruitment, promotion and discipline of personnel within the public service, including the civil service. One of its key functions is to recruit high-ranking personnel and ensure that appointments are made based on merit. This is crucial for maintaining professionalism and efficiency within the public sector.

subject: Government
q: The impartiality of the civil servants is important to the governance structure because they work for the interest of the
explanation: The impartiality of civil servants is crucial to the governance structure because they are meant to serve the state and the public, not individual political figures or parties. Civil servants must carry out their duties professionally and impartially, ensuring the continuity of government functions and the effective delivery of public services, regardless of political changes in leadership. Their primary responsibility is to serve the nation and its citizens, rather than any specific individual or political group.

subject: Government
q: Which one of the following cannot be considered as a factor that influences the delimitation of a country into constituencies?
explanation:
Political socialisation refers to the process by which individuals learn and develop political values, attitudes, and behaviours. While it influences political participation and opinions, it does not impact the delimitation of constituencies.

On the other hand, factors such as administrative convenience, geographical proximity and historical experience are directly involved in how constituencies are drawn. These factors help ensure constituencies are created in a way that makes governance efficient, manageable and reflective of existing geographic or historical boundaries.

subject: Government
q: A type of constitution suitable for the governance of a federal state is a
explanation: A rigid constitution is one that is difficult to amend because it requires a special procedure or approval from multiple levels of government. This type of constitution is suitable for federal states because it ensures that the division of powers between the central government and the states is protected from frequent or arbitrary changes. It provides stability and safeguards the autonomy of the constituent states. In contrast, a flexible constitution is easier to amend and might not provide the same level of protection for the federal structure.

subject: Government
q: A set of principles enshrined in a constitution that guide the government in making laws and policies for the welfare of the people is referred to as
explanation: Directive Principles of State Policy are a set of principles enshrined in a constitution that guide the government in making laws and policies aimed at promoting the welfare of the people. These principles are not necessarily enforceable by courts but serve as fundamental guidelines for governance. In Nigeria and India, the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) emphasise economic welfare and good governance and ensure that the government works towards improving the lives of the citizens.

subject: Government
q: Which situation in a democratic state can create an environment where military intervention is perceived as the only option to restore order, protect the population or address grievances?
explanation: In a democratic state, political crises such as political instability, government corruption, lack of effective governance or the breakdown of democratic processes can create an environment where military intervention is perceived as the only option to restore order, protect the population or address grievances. When political leaders fail to resolve issues through democratic means, people may lose faith in the political system, leading to the perception that military intervention is necessary to restore stability. While other factors like economic crises and youth unemployment can contribute to instability, political crises directly challenge the functioning of democratic institutions and can make military intervention seem like the last resort.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Despite the many challenges bedevilling the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), it has managed over the years in promoting
options: regime protection and economic stability in member countries
explanation:
Despite the many challenges faced by the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), it has successfully promoted regional integration and cooperation among its member countries. This has been achieved through efforts to foster economic collaboration, reduce trade barriers, promote peace and security and enhance political cooperation across the West African region.

ECOWAS aims to strengthen ties between member states, making them more resilient and better able to address collective challenges. This focus on integration and cooperation has been one of its central goals, despite the various hurdles it faces.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: One of the reasons for which the United Nations (UN) is considered as the only influential world body is the fact that it
options: plays a crucial role in international diplomacy
supports the war mission of member countries
influences regime change by supporting the opposition
withholds humanitarian support to military regimes
explanation: The United Nations (UN) is considered the most influential global body because it plays a crucial role in international diplomacy. It acts as a platform for dialogue and negotiations among member states. The UN helps resolve conflicts, facilitates peacekeeping operations, coordinates humanitarian aid and sets international norms and standards. This ability to bring countries together to discuss and resolve global issues is a key reason for its influence.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: The African Union (AU) aims at ensuring the following developments in member countries except for
explanation: While the African Union (AU) works to promote peace and security, social development and equal distribution of wealth in its member states, governance and democracy are more about the political systems within individual countries, and the AU’s focus is often on addressing broader issues like conflict, poverty and economic development. The AU does advocate for democratic principles, but its direct aim is not always to enforce or ensure democracy in each member state.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The Commonwealth of Nations concerns itself with one of the following activities in member countries which is
options: assisting in the overthrow of unpopular government
operating the financial sector of poor countries
explanation: The Commonwealth of Nations is primarily focused on promoting peace and security among its member countries, along with fostering cooperation in areas such as democracy, development and human rights.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: The African Union (AU) aims to promote gender equality and women's empowerment through the strategic framework organ of the
explanation: The African Union (AU) works to promote gender equality and women's empowerment through various mechanisms, including the Specialised Commission of Women Empowerment (SCWE). This body focuses on advancing the rights of women, advocating for gender equality and empowering women across the African continent.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: What encompasses a range of diplomatic, political, economic and security decisions and actions that a state embarks on to protect and promote sovereignty on the global stage?
explanation: Foreign policy refers to the range of diplomatic, political, economic and security decisions and actions that a state undertakes to protect and promote its sovereignty, interests and position on the global stage. It encompasses how a country interacts with other nations and addresses global challenges, including negotiations, treaties, trade agreements, and military actions.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: What colonial policy worked against the sovereign power and authority and the traditional political authority and institutions?
explanation:
Indirect Rule was a colonial policy implemented by the British in many parts of Africa, including Nigeria, where colonial powers governed through existing local leaders and traditional political structures. While this system appeared to preserve some local authority, it often undermined the sovereignty and true political power of the traditional institutions.

Colonial rulers maintained ultimate control and could override traditional systems when necessary, thus weakening the authority of indigenous governance.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The surge in nationalism and its related activities was fueled by oppressive colonial experiences and
options: monopoly of mineral resources and land degradation
explanation: The surge in nationalism in many African countries, particularly in West Africa, was fueled by oppressive colonial experiences and the ideology and activities of Pan-Africanism. Pan-Africanism promoted solidarity among Africans and advocated for the liberation of African nations from colonial rule. It emphasised the unity of all people of African descent, which played a significant role in galvanizing nationalist movements across the continent. Pan-African leaders, such as Kwame Nkrumah and W.E.B. Du Bois, inspired many to seek independence and resist colonial domination.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Which institution of state enables different groups and interests to be represented as well as actively participate in the political space?
explanation:
A political party enables different groups and interests to be represented and actively participate in the political space. Political parties serve as platforms through which citizens with similar ideologies, values and interests can organise and voice their concerns.

They play a crucial role in the political process by contesting elections, forming governments and influencing public policy. Political parties provide the means for citizens to engage in the democratic process and ensure that various societal interests are represented in governance.

subject: Government
q: Nationalism played a crucial role in the struggle for independence in West Africa by way of fostering a sense of community among the people and
options: abhorrence of the Western style of governance
civil disobedience and the killings of white officials
explanation:
Nationalism in West Africa played a crucial role in the struggle for independence by fostering political consciousness among the people. This heightened awareness helped citizens recognise the importance of self-rule, the need to challenge colonial powers, and the desire to reclaim their sovereignty.

Nationalist movements encouraged people to unite against colonial rule, promoting ideas of self-determination and self-governance. Political consciousness helped galvanize the masses to demand independence and participate actively in the struggle for freedom, leading to the eventual decolonization of many West African nations.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The Crown Colony policy introduced some progressive institutions of governance in West Africa such as the
options: creation of the rigid class system
introduction of the Western legal system
explanation: Under the Crown Colony policy, which was implemented by the British in various parts of their colonies in West Africa, one of the key features was the introduction of the Western legal system. This system replaced or modified many of the traditional laws and customs that existed before colonial rule. The British introduced their own legal frameworks, courts, and procedures, which were based on English law, to govern the colonies. This was part of the broader colonial policy of imposing British systems of governance, law, and administration.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The following influential nationalist leaders contributed significantly to shaping the political destiny and nation-states of West Africa except
explanation: Oliver Tambo was a South African anti-apartheid activist and leader of the African National Congress (ANC). While he played a major role in the struggle against apartheid in South Africa, he was not directly involved in the nationalist movements that led to the independence of West African nations.

subject: Government
q: Diplomacy plays a vital role in shaping the interactions between states and by extension maintaining
explanation: Diplomacy is essential in maintaining global stability because it helps resolve conflicts, fosters cooperation, and promotes peaceful interactions between states. Through negotiations, treaties, and international relations, diplomacy prevents wars, encourages economic ties, and ensures that disputes are settled through dialogue rather than force.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Military rule does not only pose a threat to democracy but also to
explanation: Military rule often leads to the suspension of fundamental human rights, such as freedom of speech, political participation, and the right to a fair trial. Military governments typically enforce strict control, limit civil liberties, and suppress opposition through censorship, arrests, or even violence.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The following constitute the key features of most independent constitutions in West African States except
explanation: Most independent constitutions in West African states were designed to promote democratic governance, national unity and socio-economic development. They established frameworks for multi-party democracy, civil rights and economic progress. However, they did not make provisions for nationalist leaders to become army commanders.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: The grave effect of the French Colonial Policy of Assimilation in her West African Colonies was the
options: shortfall in the essential needs of the colonies
slow pace in their political fortunes
waging war on neighbouring colonial territories
explanation: The French policy of Assimilation aimed to turn Africans into “Frenchmen” by imposing French culture, laws and governance structures on them. However, this policy had a negative impact on the political development of the colonies.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The British Colonial policy in her West African territories was marked by the following strategies and structures except
explanation: The British did not prioritise major investments in infrastructure unless they directly benefited colonial administration and trade (e.g., railways to transport goods to ports). Most profits from West African resources were sent to Britain rather than reinvested in local development. Education and healthcare systems were underdeveloped compared to what was available in Britain.

subject: Government
q: Globalisation impacts the development and progress of West African States by way of
explanation:
Globalisation has significantly influenced West African development through the transfer of technology, which has:

  • Improved communication and information access (e.g., mobile phones, the internet).
  • Enhanced industrial and agricultural productivity through modern tools and equipment.
  • Boosted education and healthcare with advanced research, digital learning, and medical innovations.
  • Strengthened economic growth by facilitating trade, banking, and digital financial services.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: Power, authority, legitimacy and sovereignty are described in the study of government as what?
explanation:
In the study of government, Power, Authority, Legitimacy, and Sovereignty are regarded as basic concepts and principles because they form the foundation of political science and governance.

  • Power – The ability to influence or control people and decisions.
  • Authority – The legitimate right to exercise power, often backed by law or tradition.
  • Legitimacy – The acceptance of authority by the people, giving it credibility.
  • Sovereignty – The supreme power of a state to govern itself without external interference.

subject: Government
q: A government with corrupt rulers who use their power to exploit the people and natural resources of their own territory to extend their personal wealth and political power is described as
explanation:
A kleptocracy is a government where rulers exploit national resources and use their power for personal gain rather than for the welfare of the people. In such regimes:

  • Corrupt leaders embezzle public funds and mismanage resources.
  • Government policies benefit the ruling class rather than the citizens.
  • Public institutions are weakened due to corruption and nepotism.
  • The state’s wealth is often hidden in offshore accounts or foreign investments.

subject: Government
q: In what way can the local government system assist in national development? By
options: aligning local efforts with broader national goals
depending on the central government for basic needs and development
encouraging youth movement to the industrial centres
ensuring only top-bottom policies of government are supported
explanation:
Local governments play a crucial role in national development by ensuring that grassroots efforts align with national objectives. This is done through:

  • Implementing national policies at the local level – Ensuring that development plans reach rural and urban communities.
  • Encouraging local economic growth – Supporting small businesses, agriculture, and local industries to contribute to national progress.
  • Providing essential services – Education, healthcare, infrastructure, and public utilities are crucial for national stability and growth.
  • Promoting democratic participation – Allowing citizens to engage in governance and decision-making enhances accountability and national unity.

subject: Government
q: The perceived perennial problem of the United Nations (UN) since its inception is the
options: nature of political leaders and their ages
poor constitutional provisions of member states
explanation:
Since its inception in 1945, the United Nations (UN) has faced challenges due to the vast political, economic and cultural differences among its member states. This diversity creates obstacles in:

  • Decision-making – Member states often have conflicting national interests, making it difficult to reach a consensus on global issues.
  • Peacekeeping efforts – Different political ideologies and regional conflicts hinder collective action.
  • Economic disparities – Developing nations struggle to have equal influence in global affairs.
  • Veto power of permanent Security Council members – The interests of powerful nations (e.g., the U.S., China, Russia) often override global concerns.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: For the executive to enjoy the goodwill of the people in a democratic state, it must adhere to the tenets of
explanation:
For a government to gain and maintain the goodwill of the people in a democratic state, it must uphold political equality, which ensures that:

  • All citizens have equal political rights – Every individual, regardless of background, has the right to vote, run for office, and participate in governance.
  • Laws and policies apply equally – No group should be favoured over another in decision-making.
  • Fair representation exists – Public offices and government policies should reflect the diversity of the population.
  • Transparency and accountability – A government that respects political equality listens to its citizens and remains accountable.

subject: Government
q:
SECTION A: Elements of Government

Discuss five ways in which the media can play a vital role in making democratic government work in Nigeria.
explanation:
Five Ways the Media Can Play a Vital Role in Making Democratic Government Work in Nigeria

The media is often referred to as the "fourth estate of the realm" because of its critical role in promoting democracy. In Nigeria, where democracy is still evolving, the media ensures accountability, transparency and citizen participation in governance. Here are five key ways the media contributes to making democratic government work in Nigeria:

1. Promoting Transparency and Accountability

The media serves as a watchdog, exposing corruption, mismanagement, and abuse of power by government officials. Investigative journalism helps to hold public officeholders accountable, ensuring that government actions align with the public interest. For example, media coverage of financial scandals and electoral malpractice pressures leaders to act responsibly.

2. Educating and Informing Citizens

A well-informed electorate is essential for a functioning democracy. The media provides accurate and up-to-date information about government policies, political developments, and civic responsibilities. By explaining complex policies in simple terms, the media helps citizens make informed decisions, especially during elections.

3. Providing a Platform for Public Debate and Participation

The media creates avenues for public discourse, where citizens can express their views on governance, social issues, and policy decisions. Platforms like radio talk shows, TV discussions, social media, and newspapers enable Nigerians to engage in meaningful conversations about national issues, strengthening democratic participation.

4. Acting as a Bridge Between the Government and the People

The media plays a vital role in communicating government policies to the people and relaying public opinions to the government. Through interviews, press conferences, and policy analysis, the media ensures that both government officials and citizens understand each other's concerns, fostering a responsive and people-oriented government.

5. Monitoring Elections and Democratic Processes

A democratic government relies on free, fair, and credible elections. The media plays a crucial role in covering election campaigns, reporting election irregularities, and providing real-time updates on voting processes. By acting as an independent observer, the media helps to prevent electoral fraud and strengthens public confidence in the democratic process.

subject: Government
q:
SECTION A: Elements of Government

Identify and explain five reasons for which civil society organisations are considered a major stakeholder in the governance structure of Nigeria.
explanation:
Five Reasons Civil Society Organisations (CSOs) Are Major Stakeholders in Nigeria’s Governance Structure

Civil Society Organisations (CSOs) help to promote democracy, good governance and national development in Nigeria. As independent, non-governmental entities, they help to bridge the gap between the government and the people. Here are five key reasons why CSOs are considered major stakeholders in Nigeria’s governance structure:

1. Promoting Transparency and Accountability

CSOs serve as watchdogs, monitoring government actions and policies to ensure transparency and accountability. They expose corruption, demand good governance, and push for reforms that promote efficiency and fairness in public administration. For example, organisations like the Socio-Economic Rights and Accountability Project (SERAP) have consistently challenged cases of financial mismanagement in Nigeria.

2. Advocating for Human Rights and Social Justice

CSOs play a critical role in protecting human rights and social justice by defending the rights of marginalized groups, women, children, and persons with disabilities. They challenge human rights violations, campaign for legal reforms, and offer support to victims of injustice. Groups like Amnesty International Nigeria and the CLEEN Foundation advocate for police reforms and justice for victims of abuse.

3. Enhancing Citizen Participation in Governance

CSOs empower citizens to engage in governance by educating them on their rights, responsibilities, and civic duties. They organize town hall meetings, voter education campaigns, and public debates to ensure citizens participate actively in decision-making processes. This strengthens democracy and ensures that government policies reflect the people's interests.

4. Providing Alternative Policy Ideas and Solutions

CSOs conduct research, propose policies, and engage in advocacy to improve governance and national development. They provide independent, well-researched alternatives to government policies, helping policymakers make informed decisions. Think tanks like the Centre for Democracy and Development (CDD) and BudgIT Nigeria analyse government budgets and policies, offering solutions to improve economic and social governance.

5. Supporting Social and Economic Development

Many CSOs focus on community development by providing healthcare, education, poverty alleviation programs, and humanitarian aid. They complement government efforts in sectors where public services are insufficient or inefficient. Organisations like ActionAid Nigeria and the Red Cross Society of Nigeria provide relief aid, empower women, and support rural development.

subject: Government
q:
SECTION A: Elements of Government

What five factors explain the reasons for the attack on the democratic system of government in some member sales of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS)?
explanation:
Five Factors Behind Attacks on the Democratic System in Some ECOWAS Member States

In recent years, democracy in some Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) member states has come under serious attack, leading to military coups, political instability and authoritarian rule. Several factors contribute to these challenges. Below are five key reasons:

1. Weak Democratic Institutions

Many ECOWAS states struggle with fragile democratic institutions, such as weak judicial systems, compromised electoral commissions, and ineffective parliaments. These institutions often fail to uphold democratic principles, allowing election rigging, judicial manipulation, and constitutional amendments that favour incumbents. When institutions fail to function effectively, it creates a breeding ground for political instability and unconstitutional power grabs.

2. Corruption and Poor Governance

Widespread corruption and mismanagement of resources weaken public trust in democratic governments. When leaders embezzle public funds, fail to provide basic services, and enrich themselves at the expense of citizens, the frustration often leads to civil unrest. In some cases, military interventions or violent protests emerge as a response to corrupt civilian rule, as seen in Guinea and Mali.

3. Electoral Fraud and Manipulation

Many ECOWAS states experience elections that lack credibility, with widespread allegations of voter suppression, ballot box stuffing, and post-election violence. When political leaders refuse to accept defeat, manipulate constitutions to extend their tenure, or silence opposition parties, democracy is weakened. The loss of faith in the electoral process has led to military takeovers in countries like Niger and Burkina Faso.

4. Rising Insecurity and Terrorism

West Africa faces significant security threats from terrorist groups, armed insurgencies, and banditry. When democratic governments fail to address security challenges, people begin to see the military as a better alternative for restoring order. In countries like Mali and Burkina Faso, repeated terrorist attacks and government inaction led to public support for military interventions, undermining democratic governance.

5. Foreign Influence and Geopolitical Interests

External actors, including foreign powers and regional blocs, play a role in shaping political events in ECOWAS states. Some former colonial powers have been accused of supporting certain regimes for economic and strategic interests, while others impose sanctions that further destabilize governments. Additionally, military regimes sometimes receive support from neighbouring juntas, making democratic restoration difficult.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q:
SECTION A: Elements of Government

(a) Highlight three ways in which the judicial arm of government sustains democracy.

(b) Examine three ways by which the executive arm of government sustains democracy
explanation:
(a) Three Ways the Judicial Arm of Government Sustains Democracy

  1. Upholding the Rule of Law: The judiciary ensures that all individuals, including government officials, adhere to the constitution and legal frameworks. By interpreting and enforcing laws fairly, courts prevent abuses of power and protect citizens' rights.

  2. Protecting Fundamental Human Rights: A strong judiciary safeguards freedom of speech, association, and the press, ensuring that democracy thrives. Courts hold governments accountable when they attempt to suppress civil liberties.

  3. Resolving Electoral Disputes: Courts play a key role in resolving election-related conflicts, ensuring that electoral processes remain free, fair, and credible. By adjudicating disputes fairly, they prevent political instability and maintain public confidence in democratic institutions.

(b) Three Ways the Executive Arm of Government Sustains Democracy

  1. Ensuring Good Governance and Accountability: A democratic executive promotes transparency, accountability, and efficiency in governance. By implementing policies that reflect the will of the people, the executive fosters trust in democratic processes.

  2. Respecting the Principle of Separation of Powers: The executive should function within its constitutional limits and avoid interfering with the judiciary or legislature. This allows checks and balances to operate effectively, preventing dictatorship or authoritarianism.

  3. Protecting National Security and Stability: The executive is responsible for maintaining law and order while respecting human rights. By effectively tackling security challenges like terrorism, insurgency, and electoral violence, the executive ensures a peaceful environment for democracy to flourish.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q:
SECTION B: Political and Constitutional Developments in West Africa and International Relations

Judging from the negative impacts of the major political crises in Nigeria, explain five reasons of which peace education is vital in a state.
explanation:
Five Reasons Why Peace Education is Vital in a State Like Nigeria

  1. Prevention of Political and Ethnic Conflicts: Nigeria has experienced major political crises, often fuelled by ethnic and religious differences. Peace education teaches tolerance, conflict resolution, and dialogue, helping to reduce tensions and prevent violent clashes.

  2. Promotion of National Unity and Cohesion: Peace education fosters a sense of shared identity among citizens by emphasizing values such as cooperation, respect for diversity, and patriotism. This is essential in a multi-ethnic country like Nigeria, where divisions often threaten national stability.

  3. Reduction of Electoral Violence and Political Instability: Many political crises in Nigeria stem from election-related violence and disputes. Peace education helps citizens understand the importance of democratic principles, non-violent political participation, and respect for electoral outcomes, thereby reducing electoral conflicts.

  4. Encouragement of Rule of Law and Human Rights Protection: When people are educated on peaceful coexistence, they are more likely to respect laws, democratic institutions, and human rights. This leads to a more stable society where disputes are resolved through legal means rather than violence.

  5. Enhancement of Sustainable Development: A peaceful society creates an enabling environment for economic growth, education, and social progress. Investors, businesses, and international organizations are more likely to engage with a nation that is stable, ensuring long-term development.

topics.title: Social Dynamics and Classical Political Thought

subject: Government
q:
SECTION B: Political and Constitutional Developments in West Africa and International Relations

Identify five debilitating effects of French colonial rule in French West African Colonies.
explanation:
Five Debilitating Effects of French Colonial Rule in French West African Colonies

  1. Suppression of Indigenous Political Systems: The French policy of Assimilation undermined traditional African governance structures, replacing them with French administrative systems. This disrupted indigenous leadership and weakened the ability of local rulers to govern effectively.

  2. Exploitation of Economic Resources: The French extracted raw materials like gold, cocoa, and cotton for the benefit of France, leaving the colonies economically dependent and underdeveloped. Infrastructure was built mainly to facilitate resource extraction, not local economic growth.

  3. Limited Educational and Social Development: Unlike British colonies that allowed some level of self-rule, the French strictly controlled education, producing a small elite class loyal to France while keeping the majority of the population illiterate and unskilled. This created a long-term developmental gap.

  4. Forced Labour and Heavy Taxation: The French imposed harsh forced labour policies (such as the corvée system), where Africans were required to work on colonial projects without pay. High taxation further worsened economic hardship, leading to widespread poverty and resentment.

  5. Fragmentation and Weak National Identity: The artificial boundaries drawn by the French disregarded ethnic and cultural identities, leading to divided societies with weak national consciousness. The continued influence of Francophone policies after independence further hindered regional integration.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q:
SECTION B: Political and Constitutional Development in West Africa and International Relations

As a student of Government, discuss five ways by which the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) can operate to achieve the stated objectives.
explanation:
Five Ways ECOWAS Can Operate to Achieve Its Stated Objectives

The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) was established in 1975 to promote economic integration and political stability among its 15 member states. To achieve its objectives effectively, ECOWAS can operate in the following ways:

  1. Enhancing Economic Integration and Free Trade: ECOWAS can fully implement the ECOWAS Trade Liberalisation Scheme (ETLS) to ensure free movement of goods and services across member states. This will boost intra-regional trade, reduce reliance on external markets, and strengthen West Africa’s economic stability.

  2. Strengthening Regional Security and Conflict Resolution: The ECOWAS Standby Force and Mediation Mechanisms should be actively deployed to resolve political crises, military coups, and insurgencies within the region. By strengthening the ECOWAS Early Warning System, the organization can proactively prevent conflicts before they escalate.

  3. Improving Infrastructure and Regional Connectivity: ECOWAS can expand transnational road networks, railway systems, and energy projects to improve connectivity among member states. This will facilitate faster movement of people and goods, enhance economic development, and attract foreign investment.

  4. Promoting Democratic Governance and Human Rights: ECOWAS can enforce its Protocol on Democracy and Good Governance by ensuring free and fair elections, discouraging unconstitutional changes of government, and holding leaders accountable. This will foster political stability and stronger institutions in member states.

  5. Facilitating Free Movement of Citizens and Labour Mobility: ECOWAS can enhance its free movement policy by eliminating travel restrictions, border delays, and bureaucratic bottlenecks that hinder West Africans from working and residing in other member states. A more integrated labour market will help in job creation and economic empowerment.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q:
SECTION B: Political and Constitutional Development in West Africa and International Relations

In what five ways can the pre-colonial traditional political organisation and governance in Nigeria compare with modern days of political arrangement?
explanation:
Comparison of Pre-Colonial Traditional Political Organisation and Modern Political Arrangement in Nigeria

Nigeria’s political structure has evolved significantly from the pre-colonial era, when governance was based on traditional institutions, to the modern democratic system. Here are five key ways in which they compare:

  1. Leadership Structure and Authority

    • Pre-Colonial Era: Leadership was largely monarchical (e.g., Obas, Emirs, and Chiefs), with rulers chosen through hereditary succession or selection by kingmakers.
    • Modern Era: Leadership is now democratic, with leaders elected through a popular vote based on universal suffrage. The president, governors, and lawmakers are accountable to the electorate.

  2. Legislative and Law-Making Processes

    • Pre-Colonial Era: Laws were made by traditional councils, such as the Oyo Mesi in the Oyo Empire and the Council of Elders in Igbo communities. These laws were often based on customs, religion, and oral traditions.
    • Modern Era: Laws are made by a formal legislative body (National Assembly and State Houses of Assembly) through a structured process that includes debates, bills, and presidential/gubernatorial assent.

  3. Judicial System and Administration of Justice

    • Pre-Colonial Era: Justice was administered by traditional rulers and elders, who settled disputes using customary laws, arbitration, and mediation. Punishments were often based on traditional norms and taboos.
    • Modern Era: Nigeria now has a formal judicial system with constitutional laws, courts, and legal practitioners. Justice is administered through magistrate courts, high courts, and the Supreme Court, ensuring legal processes are standardized.

  4. Participation of Citizens in Governance

    • Pre-Colonial Era: Political participation was often limited to elders, chiefs, and council members. In some societies (e.g., the Igbo), decision-making was more inclusive, but women and young people had limited roles in governance.
